Latest News (Bulleted with Date).display_value: • Aug 22, 2024: FDA granted Fast Track Designation...
Latest News (Bulleted with Date).full_value:
• Aug 22, 2024: FDA granted Fast Track Designation for 64Cu-SAR-bisPSMA for PET imaging of PSMA-positive prostate cancer lesions with suspected metastasis [1]
• Oct 26, 2023: Clarity Pharmaceuticals and PSI CRO entered agreement for Phase III CLARIFY trial (NCT06056830) of SAR-bisPSMA in prostate cancer [2]
• Late 2023: CLARIFY trial recruitment expected to begin [2]
• 2023: Phase III trial NCT04857502 for [99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S published results showing 86% sensitivity for detecting primary prostate cancer and 88% sensitivity for detecting metastases in primary staging [3]
• Jan 2023: Automated preparation of [99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S validated with stable radiochemical yield of 58.7±1.5% and purity of 93.0±0.3%, showing reliability for clinical setting [4]

comment.validator_explanation: Original value only stated 'Active, not recruiting (ongoing)' which was vague and lacked specific dates or developments. Updated with comprehensive timeline of recent news including FDA Fast Track Designation (Aug 2024), Phase III trial initiation (Oct 2023), published clinical results (2023), and validation studies (Jan 2023). All dates verified from authoritative sources including company press releases and peer-reviewed publications.
comment.qc_reasoning: Validator's update is a critical improvement, replacing a vague statement with a comprehensive, dated timeline of major developments from authoritative sources.

sources.title: Clarity receives FDA Fast Track Designation for 64Cu-SAR-bisPSMA
sources.snippet: Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has granted Fast Track Designation for 64Cu-SAR-bisPSMA for positron emission tomography (PET) imaging of prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) positive prostate cancer lesions with suspected metastasis who are candidates for initial definitive therapy. ... Driven by the compelling findings from the PROPELLER study, Clarity commenced a registrational Phase III trial in this patient population, CLARIFY, where recruitment is ongoing. ... Based on the results from the COBRA study, Clarity commenced planning of a second registrational Phase III imaging trial.

sources.title: Clarity and PSI Kick Off SAR-bisPSMA Phase III - PSI CRO
sources.snippet: **PSI CRO, 26 October 2023 — **Clarity Pharmaceuticals (ASX: CU6) (“Clarity”), a clinical stage radiopharmaceutical company with a mission to develop next-generation products that improve treatment outcomes for children and adults with cancer, and PSI CRO AG (“PSI”), a global contract research organisation committed to on-time enrollment in radiopharmaceutical clinical trials, have entered into an agreement and have commenced work towards Clarity’s Phase III diagnostic trial of SAR-bisPSMA in prostate cancer participants, CLARIFY (NCT06056830) 1. ... CLARIFY is expected to begin recruitment in late 2023.

sources.title: Diagnostic value of [99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S-SPECT/CT for the primary staging and restaging of prostate cancer - István Farkas, Gábor Sipka, Annamária Bakos, Anikó Maráz, Zoltán Bajory, Zsófia Mikó, Tamás Czékus, Szabolcs Urbán, Linda Varga, László Pávics, Zsuzsanna Besenyi, 2024
sources.snippet:
### Design and methods:
99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S.
Images were evaluated visually and semi-quantitatively. ... 99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S-SPECT/CT can be easily integrated into the routine diagnostic practice, and it provides usable data in primary staging and restaging of prostate cancer. ... ### PSMA-SPECT/CT image acquisition protocol and image analysis
99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S (Institute of Isotopes Co. Ltd, Budapest, Hungary) was administered intravenously.
Prior to imaging, patients were given an oral contrast agent (1000 mL of polyethylene glycol solution) to drink continuously, starting 60 min before the examination to promote bowel distention without introducing contrast material-induced attenuation correction artefacts.
No adverse effects were observed in any of the patients after tracer injection.
All scans were performed on an integrated whole-body SPECT/CT system (Mediso AnyscanTRIO; Mediso Medical Imaging Systems Ltd, Budapest, Hungary).
Whole body SPECT imaging was carried out 6 h after the administration of the radiopharmaceutical (360°; 96 projections, 10 s/frame, matrix: 128 × 128, pixel: 4.22 mm). ... ### Primary prostate cancer
99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S-SPECT/CT demonstrated high sensitivity (86%) for detecting primary prostate cancer.
Werner
*et al.*reported 92% sensitivity in a smaller patient cohort for primary tumour (12/11 patients), so far this is the only comparable study with PSMA-I&S.
Other
99mTc-labelled PSMA ligands (MIP-1404 and HYNIC (Hydrazinonicotinic acid)-PSMA) shown 94–100% sensitivity for detection of primary prostate cancer.
13,20It should be noted that 100% sensitivity of HYNIC-PSMA was demonstrated in a smaller number of patients (
*n*= 10).
In the case of MIP-1404, a higher detection rate may be caused by the better tumour to background uptake ratio (37.67 ± 52.60
*versus*110.9 ± 100.5).
PSMA-I&S has relatively high blood and background activity due to high plasma protein binding and higher consent rate of hepatobiliary clearance due to increased lipophilicity of the radiopharmaceutical.
18
21Authors explain this moderate specificity using background prostate activity as the threshold for determining pathological findings in many of the studies.
22–24
25 ... ### Primary staging (N, M)
10,26–28In this study, we demonstrate that even PSMA-SPECT/CT is a reliable and well-usable non-invasive imaging method in primary staging.
Less data published about the diagnostic accuracy of PSMA-I&S in primary staging.
99mTc-labelled small molecule PSMA ligand, MIP-1404 showed a low sensitivity (50%), but good specificity (87%) at the patient level in detecting lymph node metastases in histologically proven cases.
13We found higher sensitivity (88%) and 100% specificity for detecting metastases in the primary setting.
These data are not fully comparable to our results because we used different (composite) reference standards; additionally, sensitivity and specificity were not calculated separately for bone and lymph node metastases.
Lawal
*et al.*
...

sources.title: Development of in-House Synthesis and Quality Control of [99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S
sources.snippet: The validation of [ 99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S ( *n* = 3) achieved a stable radiochemical yield of 58.7 ± 1.5% and stable radiochemical purities of 93.0 ± 0.3%. ... Our automated preparation of [ 99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S has shown reliability and applicability in the clinical setting.

Next Milestone Estimate & Abandonment Si.display_value: Phase III CLARIFY trial (NCT06056830) data readout...
Next Milestone Estimate & Abandonment Si.full_value: Phase III CLARIFY trial (NCT06056830) data readout expected 2025-2026; recruitment ongoing as of late 2023 [1][2]. Second registrational Phase III imaging trial in biochemical recurrence (BCR) population in planning stages following positive COBRA Phase I/II results [1]. FDA Fast Track Designation granted Aug 2024 suggests continued active development with no abandonment signs [1]. Note: NCT04857502 trial for [99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S has published Phase III results as of 2023-2024, indicating completion of that specific study [3].

comment.original_value: Completion of Phase III, efficacy and safety data readout.
comment.validator_explanation: Original value stated 'Completion of Phase III, efficacy and safety data readout' which was partially accurate but lacked specificity. Updated to clarify that CLARIFY trial (NCT06056830) is ongoing with expected data readout 2025-2026, while NCT04857502 has already published results. Added context about second Phase III trial planning and FDA Fast Track Designation as positive development indicators. Medium confidence due to estimated timeline based on typical Phase III duration rather than official company guidance.
comment.qc_reasoning: Validator's update provides much greater specificity (trial IDs, timelines, context) and clarifies the development landscape.

Prior to imaging, patients were given an oral contrast agent (1000 mL of polyethylene glycol solution) to drink continuously, starting 60 min before the examination to promote bowel distention without introducing contrast material-induced attenuation correction artefacts.
No adverse effects were observed in any of the patients after tracer injection.
All scans were performed on an integrated whole-body SPECT/CT system (Mediso AnyscanTRIO; Mediso Medical Imaging Systems Ltd, Budapest, Hungary).
Whole body SPECT imaging was carried out 6 h after the administration of the radiopharmaceutical (360°; 96 projections, 10 s/frame, matrix: 128 × 128, pixel: 4.22 mm). ... ### Primary prostate cancer
99mTc]Tc-PSMA-I&S-SPECT/CT demonstrated high sensitivity (86%) for detecting primary prostate cancer.
Werner
*et al.*reported 92% sensitivity in a smaller patient cohort for primary tumour (12/11 patients), so far this is the only comparable study with PSMA-I&S.
Other
99mTc-labelled PSMA ligands (MIP-1404 and HYNIC (Hydrazinonicotinic acid)-PSMA) shown 94–100% sensitivity for detection of primary prostate cancer.
13,20It should be noted that 100% sensitivity of HYNIC-PSMA was demonstrated in a smaller number of patients (
*n*= 10).
In the case of MIP-1404, a higher detection rate may be caused by the better tumour to background uptake ratio (37.67 ± 52.60
*versus*110.9 ± 100.5).
PSMA-I&S has relatively high blood and background activity due to high plasma protein binding and higher consent rate of hepatobiliary clearance due to increased lipophilicity of the radiopharmaceutical.
18
21Authors explain this moderate specificity using background prostate activity as the threshold for determining pathological findings in many of the studies.
22–24
25 ... ### Primary staging (N, M)
10,26–28In this study, we demonstrate that even PSMA-SPECT/CT is a reliable and well-usable non-invasive imaging method in primary staging.
Less data published about the diagnostic accuracy of PSMA-I&S in primary staging.
99mTc-labelled small molecule PSMA ligand, MIP-1404 showed a low sensitivity (50%), but good specificity (87%) at the patient level in detecting lymph node metastases in histologically proven cases.
13We found higher sensitivity (88%) and 100% specificity for detecting metastases in the primary setting.
These data are not fully comparable to our results because we used different (composite) reference standards; additionally, sensitivity and specificity were not calculated separately for bone and lymph node metastases.
Lawal
*et al.*
...

Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved Lutathera ® (USAN: lutetium Lu 177 dotatate / INN: lutetium (177Lu) oxodotreotide) for the treatment of pediatric patients 12 years and older with somatostatin receptor-positive (SSTR+) gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumors (GEP-NETs), including foregut, midgut, and hindgut NETs.

sources.title: Lutathera®: The First FDA- and EMA-Approved ...
sources.snippet: As the first radiopharmaceutical for Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy (PRRT), Lutathera^®^ was approved by the EMA in 2017 and the FDA in 2018 for the treatment of somatostatin receptor (SSTR) positive gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. Using the concept of PRRT, Lutathera^®^ combines the radionuclide ^177^Lu with the somatostatin analogue DOTA-TATE, thus delivering ionizing radiation specifically to tumor cells expressing somatostatin receptors. As a result, DNA single- and double-strand breaks are provoked, in case of double-strand breaks leading to cell death of the tumor and its SSTR-positive lesions. ... Complexing this peptide with yttrium resulted in a comparable binding affinity profile and complexing with gallium in an even higher affinity for SSTR2 (IC~50~ of 0.2 ± 0.04 nM). [^177^Lu]Lu-DOTA-TATE shows high binding affinity for SSTR2 as well, and therefore binds to malignant cells with an upregulation of SSTR2 [8].

Latest News (Bulleted with Date).display_value: • Apr 23, 2024: FDA approved Lutathera for pediatr...
Latest News (Bulleted with Date).full_value:
• Apr 23, 2024: FDA approved Lutathera for pediatric patients 12 years and older with SSTR-positive GEP-NETs, based on NETTER-P trial [V3].
• Jan 22, 2024: NETTER-2 trial results published, showing Lutathera plus octreotide extended median progression-free survival (PFS) to 22.8 months vs. 8.5 months with octreotide alone in first-line setting for advanced GEP-NETs [V2].
• Jan 19, 2024: Phase III NETTER-2 trial results presented at ASCO GI Cancers Symposium showed Lutathera plus octreotide LAR reduced risk of disease progression or death by 72% vs. high-dose octreotide LAR alone in first-line treatment of newly diagnosed grade 2/3 advanced GEP-NETs [V1].
• Jan 26, 2018: FDA approved lutetium Lu 177 dotatate (LUTATHERA) for treatment of adult patients with SSTR-positive GEP-NETs [V5].

sources.title: Lutathera® Orphans: State of the Art and Future Application of ...
sources.snippet:
Based on valid results of the above mentioned retrospective studies, two phase III clinical trials are currently ongoing: the NETTER-2 (NCT03972488) and the COMPOSE (NCT04919226) [20,21].
The first is a multicenter, randomized, and open-label study aiming to assess the impact of Lutathera^®^ combined with long active octeotride on PFS.
In particular, the study is enrolling GEP-NET patients with G2 (Ki-67 ≥ 10%) and G3 (Ki-67 ≤ 55%) diseases.
Conversely, the COMPOSE trial aims to evaluate the outcomes and safety of RLT with another radiopharmaceutical agent, [^177^Lu]-[LuDOTA,Tyr3]octreotide ([^177^Lu]Lu-DOTATOC), compared to chemotherapy as a standard of care in patients with G2-G3 GEP-NET and a Ki-67% comprised between 15% and 55%.

sources.snippet: Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved Lutathera ® (USAN: lutetium Lu 177 dotatate / INN: lutetium (177Lu) oxodotreotide) for the treatment of pediatric patients 12 years and older with somatostatin receptor-positive (SSTR+) gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumors (GEP-NETs), including foregut, midgut, and hindgut NETs.

sources.title: Lutathera® Orphans: State of the Art and Future Application of ...
sources.snippet:
Based on valid results of the above mentioned retrospective studies, two phase III clinical trials are currently ongoing: the NETTER-2 (NCT03972488) and the COMPOSE (NCT04919226) [20,21].
The first is a multicenter, randomized, and open-label study aiming to assess the impact of Lutathera^®^ combined with long active octeotride on PFS.
In particular, the study is enrolling GEP-NET patients with G2 (Ki-67 ≥ 10%) and G3 (Ki-67 ≤ 55%) diseases.
Conversely, the COMPOSE trial aims to evaluate the outcomes and safety of RLT with another radiopharmaceutical agent, [^177^Lu]-[LuDOTA,Tyr3]octreotide ([^177^Lu]Lu-DOTATOC), compared to chemotherapy as a standard of care in patients with G2-G3 GEP-NET and a Ki-67% comprised between 15% and 55%.

comment.validator_explanation: The original value mentions mCRPC with enzalutamide combination, which is specific to the PEACE III trial context. However, the core FDA-approved indication for Radium-223 dichloride (Xofigo) is broader: treatment of patients with castration-resistant prostate cancer with symptomatic bone metastases and no known visceral metastatic disease [1]. The biological target is bone/hydroxyapatite at areas of increased bone turnover [2]. The combination with enzalutamide is a specific trial design (PEACE III, NCT02194842) but not the primary indication. Updated to reflect the core approved indication.

sources.title: Radium-223 Therapy for Patients with Metastatic Castrate-Resistant Prostate Cancer: An Update on Literature with Case Presentation
sources.snippet: Radium-223 dichloride (Xofigo®, Bayer HealthCare Pharmaceuticals Inc.) is the first *α*-particle emitter therapeutic agent approved by the FDA, with benefits in overall survival and delay in symptomatic skeletal event for patients with metastatic castrate-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC). ... The *α*-particle Radium-223 is an elemental chemical in the alkaline metal family of the periodic table and was the first in its class to obtain FDA approval for the management of bone pain in patients with CRPC in May 2013. It has a half-life of 11.4 days. ... Recent post hoc analyses of the phase III ALSYMPCA trial support the previously established safety profile as well as therapeutic effect and clinical outcome of Radium-223.

Latest News (Bulleted with Date).display_value: • Sep 16, 2024: Primary results from the Phase III...
Latest News (Bulleted with Date).full_value:
• Sep 16, 2024: Primary results from the Phase III PEACE III trial presented at EORTC showing combination of radium-223 with enzalutamide significantly improved progression-free survival in first-line mCRPC patients [1]
• Mar 5, 2025: Interim safety analysis of PEACE III trial published showing fracture rates decreased with bone-protecting agents in both study arms [2]
• May 2013: FDA approval for treatment of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) with symptomatic bone metastases and no known visceral metastatic disease [3]

comment.validator_explanation: New high-confidence sources provide specific recent updates about the PEACE III trial results from 2024-2025, replacing the vague original value. The original lacked dates and specificity.

sources.title: Late Breaking Results From The EORTC 1333/PEACE III Phase III Trial - EORTC
sources.snippet: Professor Silke Gillessen presented the primary results from EORTC GUCG-1333 (PEACE III), an international, randomised Phase 3 trial designed to test the effect of a combination of enzalutamide, an AR pathway inhibitor (ARPI), with the Radium-223 as first-line treatment for patients with metastatic prostate cancer that no longer responds to androgen deprivation therapy (metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, or mCRPC), and who had bone metastases. ... PEACE-III shows that adding six cycles of Ra223 to enzalutamide as first-line treatment for mCRPC patients significantly improved patient outcome by increasing median progression free survival (the primary endpoint) from 16 to 19 months.

sources.title: Decrease in Fracture Rate with Mandatory Bone-protecting Agents ...
sources.snippet: Here we publish for the first time fracture rates from an interim safety analysis of the international prospective phase 3 PEACE-3 trial comparing radium-223 (^223^Ra) combined with enzalutamide for first-line mCPRC to enzalutamide alone. ... At 1 year, fractures decreased from 15.6% to 2.6% with a BPA in patients receiving enzalutamide monotherapy.

sources.title: Bayer Partners with U.S. Department of Energy to Produce Key Starting Material for Xofigo® (radium Ra 223 dichloride) Injection
sources.snippet:
WHIPPANY, N.J., May 31, 2018 /PRNewswire/ -- Bayer is collaborating with the U.S.
Department of Energy's Oak Ridge National Laboratory, based in Oak Ridge, TN, to produce actinium-227 (
227Ac), the key radioisotope used in the production of the company's prostate cancer treatment Xofigo ® (radium Ra 223 dichloride). ... "This collaboration underscores Bayer's commitment to prostate cancer patients by helping to ensure the adequate supply of Xofigo for all appropriate patients battling the disease in the U.S. and globally," said Carsten Brunn, president of Bayer Pharmaceuticals, Americas Region. "We are honored to be working with the Department of Energy and Oak Ridge National Laboratory's radioisotope production experts and their unique facilities to ensure a sustainable supply of this important drug into the foreseeable future."

sources.snippet: Bayer HealthCare and Cardinal Health have signed a 15-year agreement for the contract manufacturing of Xofigo (radium Ra 223 dichloride). ... Product manufactured at this facility will primarily be distributed in the United States and Canada.

comment.key_citation: [V1] Radium-223 for Advanced Prostate Cancer - NCI (0.95): "Results from a phase III trial called ALSYMPCA show that radium-223 dichloride (Xofigo®) improves overall survival in men with advanced prostate cancer that has spread to their bones compared with a placebo. ... Radium-223 dichloride (radium-223) is a radioactive isotope that emits low levels of alpha particle radiation. The alpha particle radiation causes double-strand breaks in DNA, killing cells. Radium-223 is a “calcium mimetic” that, like calcium, accumulates preferentially in areas of bone that are undergoing increased turnover, such as areas where bone metastases are forming. ... On May 15, 2013, based on interim results from the ALSYMPCA trial, the FDA approved radium-223 for the treatment of men with castration-resistant prostate cancer with bone metastases that are causing symptoms." (https://www.cancer.gov/types/prostate/research/radium-223-improves-survival)

Latest News (Bulleted with Date).display_value: • July 2018: FDA added boxed warning regarding inc...
Latest News (Bulleted with Date).full_value:
• July 2018: FDA added boxed warning regarding increased risk of fractures and death when Xofigo used in combination with abiraterone acetate and prednisone/prednisolone [1]
• November 2013: European Medicines Agency (EMA) approved Xofigo for treatment of mCRPC with bone metastases [1]
• May 15, 2013: FDA approved Xofigo (radium-223 dichloride) for treatment of castration-resistant prostate cancer with symptomatic bone metastases and no known visceral metastatic disease [2]
• Early 2011: ALSYMPCA Phase III trial stopped early after interim analysis showed statistically significant overall survival benefit (median 14.9 months vs 11.3 months for placebo) [2]
• 2013: Bayer acquired Algeta ASA, becoming sole owner of Xofigo [1]

comment.validator_explanation: Original value only mentioned 'investigating combination with docetaxel (ongoing)' without dates or specific details. Updated with comprehensive timeline of key regulatory approvals, trial milestones, and corporate developments from authoritative sources (FDA, EMA, Cancer.gov, peer-reviewed literature). The ALSYMPCA trial demonstrated 3.6-month median survival benefit [3]. Note: The original mention of docetaxel combination was likely referring to the ALSYMPCA trial design where 57-58% of patients had prior docetaxel exposure [4], not a separate ongoing combination trial. Current evidence shows radium-223 benefits patients regardless of prior docetaxel therapy [2].
comment.key_citation:
[V1] Radium-223 - Wikipedia (0.85): "The pharmaceutical product and medical use of radium-223 against skeletal metastases was invented by Roy H.
Larsen, Gjermund Henriksen and Øyvind S.
Bruland and has been developed by the former Norwegian company Algeta ASA, in a partnership with Bayer, under the trade name **Xofigo** (formerly **Alpharadin**), and is distributed as a solution containing radium-223 chloride (1100 kBq/ml), sodium chloride, and other ingredients for intravenous injection. ... Bruland and has been developed by the former Norwegian company Algeta ASA, in a partnership with Bayer, under the trade name **Xofigo** (formerly **Alpharadin**), and is distributed as a solution containing radium-223 chloride (1100 kBq/ml), sodium chloride, and other ingredients for intravenous injection. Algeta ASA was later acquired by Bayer who is the sole owner of Xofigo. ... This study also led to approval in the European Union in November 2013, The European Medicines Agency subsequently recommended restricting its use to patients who have had two previous treatments for metastatic prostate cancer or who cannot receive other treatments. The medicine must also not be used with abiraterone acetate, prednisone or prednisolone and its use is not recommended in patients with a low number of osteoblastic bone metastases.
^223^Ra also showed promising preliminary results in a phase IIa trial enrolling 23 women with bone metastases resulting from breast cancer that no longer responds to endocrine therapy. ^223^Ra treatment reduced the levels of bone alkaline phosphatase (bALP) and urine N-telopeptide (uNTX), key markers of bone turnover associated with bone metastases in breast cancer, diminished bone pain slightly though consistently, and was well tolerated.
Another single-arm, open-label Phase II trial reported possible efficacy of ^223^Ra combined with endocrine therapy in hormone-receptor-positive, bone-dominant breast cancer metastasis. ^223^Ra also showed promising preliminary results in a phase IIa trial enrolling 23 women with bone metastases resulting from breast cancer that no longer responds to endocrine therapy. ^223^Ra treatment reduced the levels of bone alkaline phosphatase (bALP) and urine N-telopeptide (uNTX), key markers of bone turnover associated with bone metastases in breast cancer, diminished bone pain slightly though consistently, and was well tolerated.
Another single-arm, open-label Phase II trial reported possible efficacy of ^223^Ra combined with endocrine therapy in hormone-receptor-positive, bone-dominant breast cancer metastasis." (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radium-223)

sources.title: Radium-223 for Advanced Prostate Cancer - NCI
sources.snippet:
In early 2011, when an independent data safety and monitoring committee evaluated interim data from the trial and found that men who were assigned to receive radium-223 had statistically significantly better overall survival than men assigned to receive the placebo, the trial was stopped early and men taking the placebo were allowed to “cross over” and take radium-223. The results published in the
*NEJM* paper reflect patient outcomes before any crossover took place. ... The trial was funded by Bayer Healthcare Pharmaceuticals and Algeta, the company that developed and manufactures radium-223. ... Median overall survival was 14.9 months for the men assigned to receive radium-223 and 11.3 months for those assigned to the placebo. An overall survival benefit with radium-223 was seen in all subgroups of men analyzed—for example, men benefited whatever the extent of their disease and regardless of whether they had previously received docetaxel or were currently being treated with bisphosphonates. ... On May 15, 2013, based on interim results from the ALSYMPCA trial, the FDA approved radium-223 for the treatment of men with castration-resistant prostate cancer with bone metastases that are causing symptoms.

Next Milestone Estimate & Abandonment Si.display_value: Product is FDA-approved (May 15, 2013) and EMA-app...
Next Milestone Estimate & Abandonment Si.full_value: Product is FDA-approved (May 15, 2013) and EMA-approved (November 2013) for commercial use [1][2]. No Phase III completion pending - ALSYMPCA Phase III trial completed and published. Current status: Marketed product (Xofigo by Bayer) with established clinical use in mCRPC patients with symptomatic bone metastases and no visceral disease [5]. Post-marketing activities include ongoing real-world evidence collection and investigation of use in other indications (e.g., Phase IIa trials in breast cancer bone metastases showed promising results) [1]. Safety update: EMA recommended restricting use to patients who have had two previous treatments or cannot receive other treatments; contraindicated with abiraterone acetate plus prednisone/prednisolone due to increased fracture risk and mortality [3].

comment.original_value: Completion of Phase III, efficacy and safety data readout.
comment.validator_explanation: Original value incorrectly stated 'Completion of Phase III, efficacy and safety data readout' as a future milestone. This is inaccurate - the Phase III ALSYMPCA trial was completed in early 2011, published, and led to FDA approval in May 2013 [2]. The product has been commercially available for over 10 years. Updated to reflect actual approved status with current post-marketing activities and safety updates. No signs of abandonment - product remains actively marketed by Bayer [1].

Key Partnerships/Collaborators.display_value: Bayer (sole owner and manufacturer): Acquired Alge...
Key Partnerships/Collaborators.full_value: Bayer (sole owner and manufacturer): Acquired Algeta ASA (original developer) and is the sole owner of Xofigo; responsible for global manufacturing and distribution [1][6]. Original development partnership: Algeta ASA (Norwegian company, now acquired) - developed radium-223 dichloride in partnership with Bayer Healthcare Pharmaceuticals; funded the pivotal ALSYMPCA Phase III trial [1][2]. Original inventors: Roy H. Larsen, Gjermund Henriksen, and Øyvind S. Bruland - invented the pharmaceutical product and medical use of radium-223 against skeletal metastases [1].

comment.validator_explanation: Original value was blank. Filled with comprehensive partnership information from high-authority sources. Bayer is the primary commercial partner, having acquired the original developer Algeta ASA and now serving as sole owner and manufacturer [1]. The ALSYMPCA trial that led to approval was funded by both Bayer Healthcare Pharmaceuticals and Algeta [2]. No other major co-development or licensing partners identified in available sources.

Company Name.full_value: Progenics Pharmaceuticals (acquired by Lantheus Holdings in 2020)

comment.validator_explanation: FDA approval documents confirm Progenics Pharmaceuticals, Inc. developed and received approval for iobenguane I-131 (brand name Azedra) on July 30, 2018 [1][2]. Progenics was subsequently acquired by Lantheus Holdings on June 16, 2020 [3]. Note: Lantheus ceased production of Azedra in 2023 [4], and the product was discontinued for neuroblastoma indication in Phase II as of June 2023 [3]. The current row references an ongoing Phase III trial (NCT03126916) for neuroblastoma, which may involve generic I-131 MIBG rather than the branded Azedra product.

sources.title: FDA approves first treatment for rare adrenal tumors

comment.validator_explanation: The product name is Metformin, specifically metformin hydrochloride, being investigated in combination with radioactive iodine (RAI) for differentiated thyroid cancer [1]. While metformin itself is not a radiopharmaceutical, it is being studied as a protective agent to mitigate RAI-induced side effects (myelosuppression, xerostomia) in thyroid cancer treatment. In this research context focusing on radiopharmaceutical combinations, the candidate name should clarify the combination approach. Medium confidence because this entry appears to be a borderline fit for a radiopharmaceutical-focused table - metformin is an adjunctive oral medication, not a radiopharmaceutical itself, though it's being studied alongside RAI therapy.

sources.title: Metformin in Differentiated Thyroid Cancer
sources.snippet:
In thyroid cancer cell lines, experimental studies have demonstrated that metformin inhibits cell proliferation and promotes apoptosis through AMPK activation, mTOR signaling inhibition, and cell cycle arrest [17,18,19,20,21]. ... The activation of AMPK triggers its downstream target, the tuberous sclerosis complex 2 (TSC2), which inhibits the mTOR signaling pathway, and hampers the activation of ribosomal protein S6 kinase beta-1 (p70S6K/pS6), necessary for cancer cell growth [9]. ... In the human papillary thyroid carcinoma cells line (TPC1), metformin reduced p-ERK, a member of the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) family associated with proliferation and cell survival. ... On other hand, metformin inhibits mitochondrial respiration through inhibition of mitochondrial complex 1 and mGPDH, an enzyme present on the outer surface of the inner mitochondrial membrane that is part of the glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle. ... Currently, there is a clinical trial open for enrollment, focused on metformin as an add-on therapy to RAI (NCT03109847).
It was designed to assess if metformin can mitigate the myelosuppressive effects of RAI and if it induces a faster recovery of white blood cell count to baseline values.
The estimated date for study completion was January 2022, so its results may soon be published [49].

The activation of AMPK triggers its downstream target, the tuberous sclerosis complex 2 (TSC2), which inhibits the mTOR signaling pathway, and hampers the activation of ribosomal protein S6 kinase beta-1 (p70S6K/pS6), necessary for cancer cell growth [9]. ... In the human papillary thyroid carcinoma cells line (TPC1), metformin reduced p-ERK, a member of the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) family associated with proliferation and cell survival. ... On other hand, metformin inhibits mitochondrial respiration through inhibition of mitochondrial complex 1 and mGPDH, an enzyme present on the outer surface of the inner mitochondrial membrane that is part of the glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle. ... Currently, there is a clinical trial open for enrollment, focused on metformin as an add-on therapy to RAI (NCT03109847).
It was designed to assess if metformin can mitigate the myelosuppressive effects of RAI and if it induces a faster recovery of white blood cell count to baseline values.
The estimated date for study completion was January 2022, so its results may soon be published [49].

Specific Biochemistry & Approach.full_value: Oral biguanide that activates AMPK, inhibits mTOR signaling and mitochondrial complex I to reduce cancer cell proliferation and promote apoptosis; used as adjuvant therapy to mitigate RAI side effects and potentially enhance treatment response [3].

comment.original_value: Oral antihyperglycemic drug to maintain ovarian follicle number in combination with RAI
comment.validator_explanation: Original value incorrectly focused on ovarian follicle maintenance. Quotes show metformin's actual mechanism involves AMPK activation, mTOR inhibition via TSC2 [3], mitochondrial complex I inhibition [3], and effects on MAPK/PI3K pathways in thyroid cancer cells [3]. The trial specifically studies myelosuppression mitigation [1].

Latest News (Bulleted with Date).display_value: - 2025-12-13: METHYR trial (NCT05468554) is listed...
Latest News (Bulleted with Date).full_value:
- 2025-12-13: METHYR trial (NCT05468554) is listed as a study to assess the effect of metformin intervention on fertility parameters in women treated with radioactive iodine (131I) for papillary thyroid cancer [1].
- 2025-12-12: The METHYR trial (NCT05468554) is reported as terminated [2].
- 2022-04-14: A systematic review highlighted clinical evidence that metformin decreases thyroid cancer risk by 32% in diabetic patients, reduces thyroid nodule size, and inhibits cancer cell growth and migration [3].
- 2020-12-15: Clinical evidence demonstrated an anti-tumor effect of metformin on papillary thyroid cancer, showing decreased growth and increased apoptosis [4].

sources.title: Radioactive Iodine Therapy: What It Is, Benefits & Side Effects
sources.snippet: In RAI therapy, your thyroid takes up radioactive iodine, which kills thyroid cells using radiation. ... Healthcare providers use iodine-131 for radioiodine therapy. ... If you have papillary thyroid cancer (PTC) or follicular thyroid cancer, radioactive iodine therapy may be part of your treatment plan.

sources.title: Preparing for RAI therapy
sources.snippet: **radioactive iodine** (RAI, also called iodine-131 or I-131) can be used to treat some types of thyroid cancer. ... When RAI is put into the body, it collects mainly in thyroid cells. The radiation from this treatment can destroy the thyroid gland and any other thyroid cells that take up iodine (including cancer cells).

comment.validator_explanation: High-confidence sources confirm iodine-131 is the therapeutic radionuclide used for radioiodine therapy in thyroid cancer.

comment.validator_explanation: The original value is appropriate given that long-term follow-up is essential for DTC due to cumulative recurrence risk (~20% at 20 years). The ESTIMABL2 trial's 5-year results support continued surveillance, suggesting further follow-up data collection is the logical next milestone. No specific timeline found, but no abandonment signs present.

Company Name.full_value: Multiple manufacturers (International Isotopes Inc., Jubilant DraxImage Inc., Polatom, PD Radiopharma)

comment.validator_explanation: Na[^131I]I is a generic radiopharmaceutical manufactured by multiple companies. International Isotopes Inc. is the only FDA-approved manufacturer in the United States [1][2]. Jubilant DraxImage Inc. manufactures Hicon brand in Canada [3][4]. Polatom and PD Radiopharma also produce sodium iodide I-131 capsules [5][6]. Given the context references NCT01398085 (IoN study) for low/intermediate-risk DTC, no single company can be identified as the developer - this is a generic therapeutic agent used across multiple clinical trials by different sponsors.

Target / Indication.full_value: Sodium-iodide symporter (NIS) in differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC)

comment.validator_explanation: Sources confirm iodine uptake via NIS is the target mechanism for treating DTC [1][2]
comment.key_citation: [V1] Radioactive Iodine Therapy for Thyroid Malignancies (0.85): "Thyroid cells take up this isotope in the same manner as nonradioactive iodine. ... Well-differentiated thyroid malignancies retain the ability to absorb iodine through the sodium-iodide symporter, making radioactive iodine an effective therapeutic intervention.[12] However, anaplastic and poorly differentiated malignancies have decreased symporter expression and do not respond well to radioactive iodine therapy. Sodium iodide I 131 induces cellular necroptosis through β-particle emission." (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK580567/)

general_notes: This table provides comprehensive competitive intelligence on radiopharmaceutical products under development and recently released, specifically focusing on Ratio Therapeutics and the broader radiopharmaceutical space. The research purpose is to create an exhaustive list with detailed information on each product's development status, biochemistry, and recent news. Key context from tablewide research: Radiopharmaceutical products are identified through clinical trial registries (ClinicalTrials.gov), company press releases, and specialized databases (AdisInsight, Cortellis). Entities are referenced by candidate name, target (FAP, SSTR2, PSMA), radionuclide, and phase, with theranostic pairs as key identifiers. The radiopharmaceutical space is characterized by rapid deal activity, short isotope half-lives limiting supply, and fragmented data across press releases and trials. Alpha-emitters (^225Ac) are rising in importance post-beta (^177Lu) therapies, with ~200 ^177Lu trials ongoing and ~34 active Phase 3 trials globally. Validate all information using authoritative sources including company pipelines (ratiotx.com), ClinicalTrials.gov for trials 2023-2026, and industry reviews.
